content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

May 5, 1992


Earliene McCabe, Manager
Policy and Regulation Unit
California Horse Racing Board
1010 Hurley Way, Suite 190
Sacramento, CA 95815

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our Advice No. A-91-572

Dear Ms. McCabe:

You have requested advice under the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/

Your questions originate from a telephone conversation with
Linda Hayes of your staff concerning the positions designated in
the conflict of interest code for the California Horse Racing
Board (the "board").

QUESTIONS

1. Are the racing officials presently designated in the
conflict of interest code for the board "public officials" who
should be subject to the disclosure and disqualification
provisions of the Act?

2. Are the senior special investigators presently designated
in the conflict of interest code "public officials" subject to the
disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act?




1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Page Two

CONCLUSIONS

1. The racing officials2/ presently designated in the code
are not employees, officers, members or consultants of the Horse
Racing Board, and as such, are not public officials subject to the
disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act.

2. The senior special investigators are employees of the
board who participate in the decision-making process, and as such,
are public officials under the definition of the Act.

FACTS

The conflict of interest code for the California Horse Racing
Board presently enumerates the associate judge, placing judge,
starter judge, official veterinarian, racing veterinarian and
horse identifier as racing officials, as well as senior special
investigators. The history file for the code as originally
adopted does not discuss the designation of the racing officials
as an issue between the Fair Political Practices Commission and
the Horse Racing Board. It appears this designation may have been
originated by the board.

The board is now questioning whether it was appropriate for
the code to include those enumerated racing officials and senior
special investigators.

The California Horse Racing Board is a state agency which has
supervision over horse race meetings in the State as well as over
all persons or things having to do with the operation of such
meetings. Among other things, the Board is empowered to adopt
rules and regulations for the protection of the public and the
control of horse racing and parimutual wagering; administer and
enforce all laws, rules and regulations affecting horseracing and
parimutual wagering; adjudicate controversies arising from the
enforcement of those laws and regulations; and license each racing
association and all persons who participate in a horse race
meeting with parimutual wagering. (Bus. & Prof. Code Section
19404.)

The board delegates to racing "stewards" the powers and
duties necessary to enable the board to fully and effectually
carry out its responsibilities. Racing "stewards" enter into
personal service contracts with the board to carry out these
functions and they are not the subject of this advice letter. We
will address the issue of racing officials and senior special
investigators separately.

_______________________
2/     The analysis and conclusion contained in this letter do not
apply to the Stewards who are, and shall remain, covered by the
conflict of interest code for the California Horse Racing Board.

Page Three


Racing Officials

     The board licenses any person who participates in, or has
anything to do with, the racing of horses, including trainer,
owner, jockey, driver, apprentice, exercise boy, agent, trainer,
observer, stable foreman, groom, valet, veterinarian, steward,
stable watchmen, starter, timer, judge, and any other person
acting as an official at any horse racing meeting. These
officials may not participate in a horse race meeting without a
valid license. (Bus. & Prof. Code Section 19510.)

     The board may revoke the license of a racing official if that
racing official or any employee of that official has been guilty
of any dishonest practice in connection with horse racing or has
failed to comply with any condition of the licensee's license.
(Bus. & Prof. Code Section 19517.)

     In your letter, you indicated that while racing officials
must be licensed by the board, they are not employees of the
board. Racing officials receive their salary and benefits from
the racing association sponsoring a particular horse race meeting.

     In addition to applying to the board for a license to conduct
a race, every racing association must also deposit with the board
a surety bond in the amount of $100,000 or greater, as determined
by the board, to ensure payment of employee wages and benefits
including, but not limited to, health, welfare, and pension plans.
(Bus. & Prof. Code Section 19464.)

     You indicate in your letter that these officials, on
occasion, may participate in the decision making process of a
racing steward.     On February 25, 1992, John Schillan, a
supervising special investigator for the board, clarified this by
indicating that it is the licensed function of all racing
officials to report improprieties or irregularities to the
stewards.

Senior Special Investigators

     Senior special investigators are civil service
classifications with the State of California. They conduct field
investigations to detect or verify suspected violations of the
board's laws, rules or regulations. They serve as fact finders
and report their findings in writing to the board of Stewards or
to a supervising investigator. Based on the facts contained in
the investigator's report, decisions are made by either a steward
or a supervising investigator. It is your opinion that the senior
special investigators are not involved in making decisions nor do
they recommend action to the decision makers.

Page Four


You provided the duty statement for the senior special
investigator. This statement provides, in part, that the
investigator: "...conducts independent field investigations to
detect or verify suspected violations of laws, rules, and
regulations in the preparation of cases for administrative
hearings and criminal and civil prosecutions in
more difficult or confidential investigations; gathers and
preserves evidence; testifies."

John Schillan elaborated the duties of the senior special
investigator by explaining that these individuals investigate a
broad range of interests, including but not limited to, unlicensed
employees, bookmaking, mistreatment of horses, illegal aliens,
race fixing, narcotics, financial complaints, illegal substances
in horses, etc. He indicated that the investigator recommends a
course of action to the steward, including recommended
disciplinary action and testifies at administrative and criminal
hearings. He confirmed that the senior special investigators
through their recommendations and testimony definitely participate
in the decision-making process.

ANALYSIS

Section 87300 requires every agency to adopt and promulgate a
conflict of interest code. A conflict of interest code is a set
of rules or regulations which specifically designates positions
within the agency which make or participate in the making of
decisions which may foreseeably have a material effect on any
financial interest. For each enumerated position, the code must
set out the specific types of investments, business positions,
interests in real property and income which are reportable.
(Sections 82014, 87302.)

The term "designated employee" includes:

...any officer, employee, member or consultant of any
agency involved in the making or participating in the
making of decisions which may foreseeably have a
material effect on any financial interest.

"Public official" is defined in Section 82048 as:

...every member, officer, employee or consultant of a
state or local government agency....

"State agency" is defined in Section 82049 as:

...every state office, department, division, bureau, board
and commission, and the Legislature.

Page Five


     Clearly, the California Horse Racing Board is a state agency.
The question here is whether or not the racing officials are
employees, officers, members or consultants of the board involved
in the making or participating in the making of decisions which
may foreseeably have a material effect on any financial interest.

     A public official "makes a governmental decision" when he or
she, acting within the authority of his or her office:

     (1)  Votes on a matter;

     (2)  Appoints a person;

     (3)  Obligates or commits his or her agency to any
          course of action;

     (4)  Enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of
          his or her agency;

     (5)  Determines not to act, within the meaning of
          sub-paragraphs (1), (2), (3) and (4), unless such
          determination is made because of his or her
          financial interest.

                              Regulation 18700(b)

     A public official "participates in the making of a
governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or
her position, he or she:

     (1)  Negotiates, without significant substantive review,
          with a governmental entity or private person
          regarding the decision; or

     (2)  Advises or makes recommendations to the decision-
          maker, either directly or without significant
          substantive review, by:

          (A)  Conducting research or making any
               investigation which requires the
               exercise of judgment on the part of the
               official and the purpose of which is to
               influence the decision; or

          (B)  Preparing or presenting any report,
               analysis or opinion, orally or in
               writing, which requires the exercise of
               judgment on the part of the official
               and the purpose of which is to
               influence the decision.

                              Regulation 18700(c)

Page Six


While racing officials must be licensed by the board, they
are employed by the racing association conducting the race. It
appears from the information available, that racing officials are
employees of the respective racing association and are not
employees of the board.

Are the racing officials then "consultants" to the Horse
Racing Board?

The term "designated employee" includes any officer,
employee, member or consultant of any agency involved in the
making or participation in the making of decisions which may
foreseeably have a material effect on any financial interest.
(Section B2019 (emphasis added).)

A "consultant" is a natural person who provides, under
contract, information, advice, recommendation or counsel to a
governmental agency. (Regulation 18700(a)(2).) The term
"consultant" does not include a person who:

(A) Conducts research and arrives at conclusions
with respect to his or her rendition of information
advice, recommendation or counsel independent of the
control and direction of the agency or any agency
official, other than normal contract monitoring, and

(B) Possesses no authority with respect to any
agency decision beyond the rendition of information
advice, recommendation or counsel.

Regulation 18700(a)(2)(A)(B)

The board regulates the activities of individuals wishing to
become employed at horse race meetings by requiring that they
become licensed. Racing officials are in an occupation which
requires licensing in a manner similar to nurses,
contractors, tax preparers, veterinarians, etc. A racing
association cannot employ any racing official unless that official
has a current and valid license issued by the board. The racing
officials are not under contract with the board. Further, they
are not making recommendations or providing advice to the board.

Thus, it would be appropriate for the California Horse Racing
Board to delete the Racing Officials from its code.

With respect to the senior special investigators, these
positions are employees of the board and do make or participate in
the making of decisions. They are making a determination whether
a violation may have occurred and are reporting their findings to
the Board of Stewards. In making their report, they can recommend
a disciplinary course of action to be taken in a specific case.

Page Seven


They also provide testimony in criminal or civil cases and in
these ways can make or participate in the making of governmental
decisions. It would not be appropriate to delete the senior
special investigators from the conflict of interest code.

I hope this answers your questions sufficiently. If I can be
of any further assistance, please do not hesitate to contact me at
(916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division

JET/jt

2. Are the senior special investigators presently designated in the conflict of interest code "public officials" subject to the disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act?

1, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

The history file for the code as originally adopted does not discuss the designation of the racing officials as an issue between the Fair Political Practices Commission and the Horse Racing Board. It appears this designation may have been originated by the board.

(Bus. & Prof. Code Section 19404.)

The board delegates to racing "stewards" the powers and duties necessary to enable the board to fully and effectually carry out its responsibilities. Racing "stewards" enter into personal service contracts with the board to carry out these functions and they are not the subject of this advice letter. We will address the issue of racing officials and senior special investigators separately.

2/ The analysis and conclusion contained in this letter do not apply to the Stewards who are, and shall remain, covered by the conflict of interest code for the California Horse Racing Board.

Racing Officials

These officials may not participate in a horse race meeting without a valid license. (Bus. & Prof. Code Section 19510.)

In your letter, you indicated that while racing officials must be licensed by the board, they are not employees of the board. Racing officials receive their salary and benefits from the racing association sponsoring a particular horse race meeting.

In addition to applying to the board for a license to conduct a race, every racing association must also deposit with the board a surety bond in the amount of $100,000 or greater, as determined by the board, to ensure payment of employee wages and benefits including, but not limited to, health, welfare, and pension plans. It is your opinion that the senior special investigators are not involved in making decisions nor do they recommend action to the decision makers.

You provided the duty statement for the senior special investigator. This statement provides, in part, that the investigator: "...conducts independent field investigations to detect or verify suspected violations of laws, rules, and regulations in the preparation of cases for administrative hearings and criminal and civil prosecutions ... participates in more difficult or confidential investigations; gathers and preserves evidence; testifies."

John Schillan elaborated the duties of the senior special investigator by explaining that these individuals investigate a broad range of interests, including but not limited to, unlicensed employees, bookmaking, mistreatment of horses, illegal aliens, race fixing, narcotics, financial complaints, illegal substances in horses, etc. He indicated that the investigator recommends a course of action to the steward, including recommended disciplinary action and testifies at administrative and criminal hearings. sections.analysis:
Section 87300 requires every agency to adopt and promulgate a conflict of interest code. A conflict of interest code is a set of rules or regulations which specifically designates positions within the agency which make or participate in the making of decisions which may foreseeably have a material effect on any financial interest. For each enumerated position, the code must set out the specific types of investments, business positions, interests in real property and income which are reportable. (Sections 82014, 87302.)

The term "designated employee" includes:

...any officer, employee, member or consultant of any agency involved in the making or participating in the making of decisions which may foreseeably have a material effect on any financial interest.

"Public official" is defined in Section 82048 as:

...every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency....

"State agency" is defined in Section 82049 as:

...every state office, department, division, bureau, board and commission, and the Legislature.

Clearly, the California Horse Racing Board is a state agency. The question here is whether or not the racing officials are employees, officers, members or consultants of the board involved in the making or participating in the making of decisions which may foreseeably have a material effect on any financial interest.

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when he or she, acting within the authority of his or her office:

(1) Votes on a matter;

(2) Appoints a person;

(3) Obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action;

(4) Enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency;

(5) Determines not to act, within the meaning of sub-paragraphs (1), (2), (3) and (4), unless such determination is made because of his or her financial interest.

Regulation 18700(b)

A public official "participates in the making of a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her position, he or she:

(1) Negotiates, without significant substantive review, with a governmental entity or private person regarding the decision; or

(2) Advises or makes recommendations to the decision-maker, either directly or without significant intervening substantive review, by:

(A) Conducting research or making any investigation which requires the exercise of judgment on the part of the official and the purpose of which is to influence the decision; or

(B) Preparing or presenting any report, analysis or opinion, orally or in writing, which requires the exercise of judgment on the part of the official and the purpose of which is to influence the decision.

Regulation 18700(c)

While racing officials must be licensed by the board, they are employed by the racing association conducting the race. It appears from the information available, that racing officials are employees of the respective racing association and are not employees of the board.

Are the racing officials then "consultants" to the Horse Racing Board?

The term "designated employee" includes any officer, employee, member or consultant of any agency involved in the making or participation in the making of decisions which may foreseeably have a material effect on any financial interest. (Section 82019 (emphasis added).)

A "consultant" is a natural person who provides, under contract, information, advice, recommendation or counsel to a governmental agency. (Regulation 18700(a)(2).) The term "consultant" does not include a person who:

(A) Conducts research and arrives at conclusions with respect to his or her rendition of information, advice, recommendation or counsel independent of the control and direction of the agency or any agency official, other than normal contract monitoring, and

(B) Possesses no authority with respect to any agency decision beyond the rendition of information, advice, recommendation or counsel.

Regulation 18700(a)(2)(A)(B)

The board regulates the activities of individuals wishing to become employed at horse race meetings by requiring that they become licensed. Racing officials are in an occupation which requires licensing in a manner similar to barbers, nurses, contractors, tax preparers, veterinarians, etc. A racing association cannot employ any racing official unless that official has a current and valid license issued by the board. The racing officials are not under contract with the board. Further, they are not making recommendations or providing advice to the board.

Thus, it would be appropriate for the California Horse Racing Board to delete the Racing Officials from its code.

With respect to the senior special investigators, these positions are employees of the board and do make or participate in the making of decisions. They are making a determination whether a violation may have occurred and are reporting their findings to the Board of Stewards. In making their report, they can recommend a disciplinary course of action to be taken in a specific case.

They also provide testimony in criminal or civil cases and in these ways can make or participate in the making of governmental decisions. It would not be appropriate to delete the senior special investigators from the conflict of interest code.

I hope this answers your questions sufficiently. If I can be of any further assistance, please do not hesitate to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

CONCLUSION: 1. The racing officials2/ presently designated in the code are not employees, officers, members or consultants of the Horse Racing Board, and as such, are not public officials subject to the disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act.

2. The senior special investigators are employees of the board who participate in the decision-making process, and as such, are public officials under the definition of the Act.
